answer: The structure of Nivkh words and sentences for requests is ñi (I need) + det (this = to- or ha-, that = ha- or to-, a = zero) + poss (my = zero, your = ta-, own = ni-) + adj (big = pilo-, winter = ne- or p'e) + noun root + loc (over there = -x) + ekid (request suffix). Modifiers are prefixed in the order det-poss-adj-noun-loc-ekid. The p', t', k' are single sounds (affricates or ejectives), and fx/sx clusters are avoided by language rules, so no such combinations occur in roots or suffixes. Sentences are simple subject-object-verb like , but here the object is modified as above.

The loc -x in xekid endings for over there items. The given nouns in M2 confirm roots like flang, tus , hajk , zrovs , tif . The matching in M1 and translations in M2/M3 confirm the order and components. No internet used, all from data patterns.
